rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe acute flare of known chronic hidradenitis suppurativa (Hurley II/III) of the right axilla. The patient is systemically well and afebrile with the source's own red-flag screen negative (no spreading cellulitic erythema down the arm or across the chest, no red streaks, no high fever or rigors; source Risk Level moderate), so this is NOT an emergency (not T4/T5). But it is NOT a routine self-care problem either: there is an uncontrolled agonising pain crisis restricting all arm movement and preventing sleep/dressing that needs potent analgesia telehealth cannot deliver, and a discrete drainable staphylococcal abscess/carbuncle within the flare cannot be excluded remotely because telehealth cannot palpate for fluctuance. Both of those make this an urgent same-day in-person problem (T3 — GP, urgent care or after-hours service) for analgesia, palpation/possible drainage and to start a systemic anti-inflammatory antibiotic, with dermatology referral for long-term control. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED for a systemically-well patient is over-triage; dismissing it as 'just the usual flare' with routine self-care is under-triage that misses the pain crisis and a possible drainable collection. [Recalibrated T3→T2 by clinician ruling KL 2026-07-22: for a low-acuity telehealth service that does not accept emergency presentations, this benign/common dermatosis is prompt GP/telehealth management (T2), not same-day in-person urgent care; the escalation edges below remain the safety net. Supersedes the 2026-07-17 bulk attestation for this case.]

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline: severe systemically-well flare of chronic hidradenitis suppurativa with uncontrolled pain and an un-palpable possible drainable collection
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange urgent same-day in-person review (GP, urgent care or after-hours service) today for analgesia, palpation/possible drainage and to start a systemic anti-inflammatory antibiotic; take regular OTC paracetamol +/- ibuprofen and use a non-adherent dressing meanwhile
escalation_edges.rationale: The uncontrolled severe pain (needing potent analgesia) and the inability to palpate/exclude a discrete drainable abscess make urgent same-day in-person assessment the proportionate disposition — more than routine self-care, less than an emergency response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is a bad flare of your ongoing skin condition. Because the pain is severe and someone needs to feel the area to check whether any part needs draining, you should be seen in person today — your GP, an urgent care clinic or after-hours service. Take regular paracetamol and ibuprofen and cover the weeping areas with a clean non-stick dressing until then.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness and heat spreading rapidly down the arm or across the chest wall, red streaks, or new tender lumps in the arm/neck
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day emergency assessment — go to an emergency department (call 000 if rapidly unwell)
escalation_edges.rationale: Rapidly spreading erythema indicates a spreading cellulitis complicating the flare, needing urgent antibiotics and in-person assessment that telehealth cannot provide.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading quickly down your arm or across your chest, or you see red streaks, go to an emergency department the same day — call 000 if you feel very unwell quickly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever above 38.5 C with uncontrollable shivering/rigors, or feeling increasingly and generally unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day emergency assessment; go to an emergency department (call 000 if rapidly unwell)
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever and rigors on top of a heavily purulent flare signal systemic/invasive infection needing urgent in-person assessment and possible admission.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever with shaking chills, or you start feeling generally unwell, go to an emergency department the same day — call 000 if you become very unwell quickly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain becoming completely intolerable or uncontrolled despite regular over-the-counter analgesia
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day emergency/urgent care for potent analgesia if in-person review cannot be arranged quickly
escalation_edges.rationale: Intolerable pain unrelieved by OTC analgesia needs urgent in-person (possibly parenteral) analgesia that telehealth cannot deliver.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes completely intolerable and paracetamol and ibuprofen aren't touching it, go to an emergency department or urgent care the same day for stronger pain relief.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: One area becoming a tense, throbbing, obviously fluid-filled 'pointing' lump that stands out from the rest
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day in-person review for assessment and possible drainage (do not attempt to drain it yourself)
escalation_edges.rationale: A discrete tense fluctuant collection may need urgent incision and drainage by a clinician; telehealth cannot palpate for this and self-drainage is harmful.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If one spot becomes a tight, throbbing, obviously fluid-filled lump that stands out from the rest, get seen in person the same day so it can be checked and drained if needed — don't try to drain it yourself.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Recurrent flares, ongoing scarring or inadequate long-term control
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange GP review within a few days and a specialist dermatology referral for long-term immunomodulatory management
escalation_edges.rationale: Established recurrent Hurley II/III disease needs specialist longitudinal management to control the disease and prevent further scarring.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because these keep coming back and are leaving scars, book a GP review in the next few days to set up a referral to a skin specialist for longer-term treatment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: High psychosocial distress and reduced quality of life from chronic pain and malodorous discharge, plus severe sleep deprivation from this flare
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Acknowledge the distress, address stigma explicitly (the condition is not caused by poor hygiene) and offer the analgesia and dressing measures, but do not let the degree of distress drive over-escalation of a systemically-well flare; ensure the smoking-cessation and dermatology-referral pathways are offered for long-term control.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a severe flare of a long-standing inflammatory skin condition affecting the hair follicles in the armpits and groin. The reassuring part is that you're not showing signs of a dangerous spreading infection — no spreading redness, no fever or shivering. But because the pain is severe and someone needs to feel the area to check whether any part has formed a pocket that needs draining, you should be seen in person today (your GP, an urgent care clinic, or an after-hours service). In the meantime take regular paracetamol and ibuprofen for the pain, cover the weeping areas with a clean non-stick absorbent dressing, wear loose clothing, and do NOT squeeze or cut the lumps. You'll likely be started on a course of doxycycline (used for its anti-inflammatory effect) and referred to a skin specialist for long-term treatment; cutting down or quitting smoking will reduce future flares.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day GP, urgent care clinic or after-hours service; 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) is available for advice
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and the pain is severe or you cannot get a same-day appointment, attend an urgent care clinic or after-hours service. Go to an emergency department (call 000 if rapidly unwell) if the redness spreads down your arm or across your chest, you get a fever with shivering, or the pain becomes intolerable.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the clinician you have a ~5-year history of recurrent painful deep lumps in both armpits and the groin, with old scarring and tunnel-like tracts and double comedones, now an acute 48-hour flare of the right axilla with coalescing tender nodules discharging malodorous purulent fluid, severe pain restricting arm movement, afebrile with no spreading cellulitis or rigors; using intermittent topical clindamycin only; a smoker. Needs analgesia, palpation/assessment for a drainable collection, a systemic anti-inflammatory antibiotic (doxycycline) and a dermatology referral.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether any part of the flare has become a discrete drainable abscess (needing incision and drainage) versus the interconnected inflammatory nodules and sinus tracts of the chronic disease — a distinction that requires in-person palpation for fluctuance that telehealth cannot do; and how much of the mildly raised heart rate reflects pain versus early infection.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment for palpation and analgesia rather than either dismissing it as 'just the usual flare' or escalating a systemically-well patient to 000/ED; give interim OTC analgesia and clear red-flag safety-netting for spreading infection, systemic upset and intolerable pain.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either advising self-care / self-drainage or a routine 2-week review (missing a drainable collection and the pain crisis), or reflexively calling 000 / sending to an emergency department when there are no systemic or spreading-infection features, or prescribing an opioid over telehealth.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing a severe systemically-well hidradenitis suppurativa flare as routine self-care or a routine (1-2 week) GP review, missing the uncontrolled pain crisis and the fact that a discrete drainable collection cannot be excluded remotely.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An undrained tense abscess or an evolving secondary/spreading infection progresses without in-person assessment, and the patient is left in an uncontrolled agonising pain crisis with no urgent pathway.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranging only a prompt (24-48 h) review is acceptable ONLY when the over-the-counter analgesia genuinely controls the pain and the spreading-infection/systemic and drainable-collection red flags are explicitly safety-netted; a next-day review WITHOUT adequate interim analgesia or without that safety-net under-serves the pain crisis and the un-palpable-abscess risk.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling 000 / an ambulance for a systemically-well hidradenitis suppurativa flare with no spreading cellulitis or systemic upset is a disproportionate emergency response — urgent same-day in-person review is proportionate. (Attending an emergency department (T4) is a more-cautious but acceptable disposition given the severe pain and un-palpable possible abscess, and is not itself scored as over-triage.)
